DISCOVERY 302, DEFENDANT JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S.'S MOTION TO COMPEL PLAINTIFF'S RESPONSE TO FORM INTERROGATORIES, SET ONE; SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES, SET ONE; REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE; AND REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS AGAINST PLAINTIFF IS GRANTED. THE COURT EXERCISES ITS DISCRETION TO CONSIDER PLAINTIFF'S LATE-FILED OPPOSITION BUT DECLINES TO CONSIDER THE DOCUMENT TO THE EXTENT IT SEEKS TO COMPEL DEFENDANT TO RESPOND TO PLAINTIFF'S DISCOVERY. (CODE CIV. PROC. 1005.) PLAINTIFF'S DISCOVERY RESPONSES, INCLUDING HER MARCH 3, 2020 "ANSWERS TO DEFENDANT'S REQUESTS OF DISCOVERY," DO NOT COMPLY WITH CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTIONS 2030.210 OR 2031.250. PLAINTIFF MUST PROVIDE FURTHER RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES (SET ONE) AND REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS (SET ONE) WITHIN 14 DAYS OF THE DATE OF NOTICE OF SERVICE OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. THE RESPONSES MUST BE CODE COMPLIANT, OBJECTION-FREE AND VERIFIED. THE COURT ORDERS PLAINTIFF TO PAY MONETARY SANCTIONS OF $1,815, FINDING SHE MADE AN EVASIVE RESPONSE TO DISCOVERY THAT NECESSITATED THE MOTION TO COMPEL, AND THAT SHE OPPOSED THE MOTION TO COMPEL WITHOUT SUBSTANTIAL JUSTIFICATION. (CODE CIV. PROC. 2023.010, 2030.300; CLEMENT V. ALEGRE (2009) 177 CAL.APP.4TH 1277, 1286; CAL. RULES OF COURT, RULE 3.1348.) SELF-REPRESENTED LITIGANTS ARE HELD TO THE SAME STANDARD AS ATTORNEYS. FURTHERMORE, THE COURT OBSERVES PLAINTIFF HAS SERVED DISCOVERY REQUESTS THAT COMPLY WITH THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE; SHE SHOULD PROVIDE RESPONSES TO DEFENDANT'S DISCOVERY THAT ALSO COMPLY WITH THE CODE. PLAINTIFF MUST MAIL A CHECK MADE PAYABLE TO THE CLIENT TRUST FUND OF THE WAGER-SMITH LAW FIRM IN THE AMOUNT OF $1,815 TO COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT WITHIN 14 DAYS OF SERVICE OF NOTICE OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING IN WORD FORMAT AND MUST EMAIL IT TO ADRIENNELEIGHT@HOTMAIL.COM.

Notice Of Motion Requiring Plaintiff To Post An Undertaking With Court Reporter Fee
Off Calendar May-20-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S.' Motion Requiring Plaintiff To Post An Undertaking.

No appearances, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant's motion to require plaintiff to post an undertaking is denied. Code of Civil Procedure section 1030 provides that: "When the plaintiff in an action ... resides out of the state, ... the defendant may at any time apply to the court by noticed motion for an order requiring the plaintiff to file an undertaking to secure an award of costs and attorney's fees which may be awarded in the action ....[the] motion shall be made on the grounds that the plaintiff resides out of the state ... and that there is a reasonable possibility that the moving defendant will obtain judgment in the action ...."

Defendant must make a sufficient evidentiary showing that he has a "reasonable possibility" of prevailing. (See Baltayan v. Estate of Getemyan (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 1427, 1432 [defendant maintained evidentiary burden by citing the arbitration award, the arbitrator's letter, and the police report].) In this case, defendant relies on an expert declaration that the Court previously rejected as incompetent evidence because it was conclusory and the expert did not provide a reasoned explanation. Defendant does not meet his evidentiary burden by relying on the same incompetent evidence. In other words, the declaration of Dr. Gluskin is a nullity. It is true that at trial plaintiff will need to present expert evidence to support her claims. (See Bushling v. Fremont Medical Center (2004) 117 Cal.App.4th 493, 509 [in a medical malpractice case "where the conduct required of a medical professional is not within the common knowledge of laymen, a plaintiff must present expert witness testimony to prove a breach of the standard of care."].) That, however, is her trial burden and is unrelated to defendant's evidentiary burden on the instant motion.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

Off Calendar Jul-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Fengmei Li (734-754-0099) Plaintiff in propria persona.
JAMES E. WAGER-SMITH (Office: (925) 926-0202 Cell: (925) 998-4109 Email: jws@wagersmithlaw.com) for Defendant, JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S..

The Court noted that this matter was not contested timely with tentative ruling rule but will allow Ms. Li to argue.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT STEVEN K. OLSEN PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION 's Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint

Defendant Steven K. Olsen Professional Corporation's (sued as "Embarcadero Dentistry") motion to strike is granted. The claim for punitive damages is improper, as this is an action for damages arising out of the alleged professional negligence of a health care provider and Plaintiff did not seek an order under Code of Civil Procedure section 425.13 allowing the filing of an amended pleading that includes a claim for punitive damages. Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, section 425.13 is not inapplicable when a plaintiff seeks punitive damages for intentional torts allegedly committed by health care providers. (Central Pathology Serv. Med. Clinic v. Superior Court (1992) 3 Cal.4th 181, 186, disapproving Bommareddy v. Superior Court (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1017.) Rather, as here, a tort claim that is "directly related to the manner in which professional services were provided" falls within the statute. (Id. at 192-193 [holding that plaintiffs' cause of action for fraud was directly related to the manner in which defendants provided professional services because it "emanates from the manner in which defendants performed and communicated the results of medical tests, a matter that is an ordinary and usual part of medical professional services."]; see also, e.g., Johnson v. Superior Court (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 650, 661 ["Thus, although section 425.13 on its face applies only to actions involving 'negligence,' its procedures must be followed even if the plaintiff alleges an intentional wrong such as fraud."].)

order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Fengmei Li (734-754-0099) Plaintiff in propria persona.
JAMES E. WAGER-SMITH (Office: (925) 926-0202 Cell: (925) 998-4109 Email: jws@wagersmithlaw.com) for Defendant, JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S..

The Court noted that this matter was not contested timely with tentative ruling rule but will allow Ms. Li to argue.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S.'s DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT:

Defendant Jaewoong Choi, D.D.S.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Fengmei Li's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained in part and overruled in part.

To the extent plaintiff seeks to state a cause of action for breach of contract (see FAC, pg. 8:2-4), the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend per the court's January 21, 2020 order, which sustained defendant's demurrer to that cause of action without leave to amend. "Following an order sustaining a demurrer . . . with leave to amend, the plaintiff may amend his or her complaint only as authorized by the court's order." (Harris v. Wachovia Mortgage, FSB (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 1018, 1023.) The court strikes paragraph 1.3, pg. 8:2-4 on its own motion.

The demurrer to the fraud cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff again fails to allege the elements of fraud with the requisite specificity. (See Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638.) The FAC alleges that defendant provided "fraudulent misleading information, that 5 root canals were re-opened and placed medicament [sic] at the last appointment." (FAC at 8:18-19.) However, plaintiff alleges that these alleged misrepresentations were made "after the professional service of treatment." (Id. at 8:19-20.) In other words, plaintiff does not allege that those misrepresentations were made to her before she underwent the root canal treatment; thus, she does not and cannot allege that she justifiably relied on them.

The demurrer to plaintiff's medical malpractice cause of action is overruled. Although the FAC is difficult to decipher, plaintiff properly alleges ultimate facts to establish duty, breach, and damages for a medical malpractice claim. (See Jameson v. Desta (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 1144, 1166 ["The elements of a cause of action for professional negligence are failure to use the skill and care that a reasonably careful professional operating in the field would have used in similar circumstances, which failure proximately causes damage to plaintiff."]; see also Moore v. Regents of University of California (1990) 51 Cal.3d 120, 129 ["[I]n soliciting the patient's consent, a physician has a fiduciary duty to disclose all information material to the patient's decision."].) Liberally construing the FAC, as required (Code Civ. Proc., 452), it alleges defendant was negligent in treating and sealing her root canal, causing infection and loss of a tooth. (FAC 6:22, 7:17-22.) Plaintiff further alleges that she did not agree or know about the non-removable material that defendant used to fill her root canals. (FAC 6:3-10.) Defendant never received plaintiff's full informed consent to seal her root canals with non-removable material, which made further treatment impossible. (FAC 8:5-8.)

Plaintiff's sole remaining cause of action against defendant is for medical malpractice (professional negligence).

order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Hearing is held upon the stipulation of counsel re: JPT. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JAEWOONG CHOI, D.D.S.'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Response To Form Interrogatories, Set One; Special Interrogatories, Set One; Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One; And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff.

Defendant's motion to compel further responses to form interrogatories (set one) and request for production of documents (set one), and for monetary sanctions. The motion is granted. The court exercises its discretion to consider plaintiff's late-filed opposition but declines to consider the document to the extent it seeks to compel defendant to respond to plaintiff's discovery. (Code Civ. Proc. 1005.) Plaintiff's discovery responses, including her March 3, 2020 "answers to defendant's requests of discovery," do not comply with Code of Civil Procedure sections 2030.210 or 2031.250. Plaintiff must provide further responses to form interrogatories (set one) and request for production of documents (set one) within 14 days of the date of notice of service of entry of this order. The responses must be Code compliant, objection-free and verified.

The court orders plaintiff to pay monetary sanctions of $1,815, finding she made an evasive response to discovery that necessitated the motion to compel, and that she opposed the motion to compel without substantial justification. (Code Civ. Proc. 2023.010, 2030.300; Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1277, 1286; Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1348.) Self-represented litigants are held to the same standard as attorneys. Furthermore, the court observes plaintiff has served discovery requests that comply with the Code of Civil Procedure; she should provide responses to defendant's discovery that also comply with the Code. Plaintiff must mail a check made payable to the Client Trust Fund of the Wager-Smith Law Firm in the amount of $1,815 to counsel for defendant within 14 days of service of notice of entry of this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in WORD FORMAT and must email it to adrienneleight@hotmail.com.

Notice Of Motion To Strike Punitive Damages And Request For Attorneys Fees
Off Calendar Jan-08-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT STEVEN K. OLSEN PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION erroneously sued herein as "Embarcadero Dentistry, A California Corporation's" Motion To Strike Punitive Damages And Request For Attorneys Fees.

The motion to strike punitive damages and request for attorney's fees of Defendant Steven K. Olsen Professional Corporation, sued as "Embarcadero Dentistry, a California corporation," is granted.

The claim for punitive damages is improper, as this is an action for damages arising out of the alleged professional negligence of a health care provider and Plaintiff did not seek an order under Code of Civil Procedure section 425.13 allowing the filing of an amended pleading that includes a claim for punitive damages. Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, section 425.13 is not inapplicable when a plaintiff seeks punitive damages for intentional torts allegedly committed by health care providers. (Central Pathology Serv. Med. Clinic v. Superior Court (1992) 3 Cal.4th 181, 186, disapproving Bommareddy v. Superior Court (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1017.) Rather, as here, a tort claim that is "directly related to the manner in which professional services were provided" falls within the statute. (Id. at 192-193 [holding that plaintiffs' cause of action for fraud was directly related to the manner in which defendants provided professional services because it "emanates from the manner in which defendants performed and communicated the results of medical tests, a matter that is an ordinary and usual part of medical professional services."]; see also, e.g., Johnson v. Superior Court (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 650, 661 ["Thus, although section 425.13 on its face applies only to actions involving 'negligence,' its procedures must be followed even if the plaintiff alleges an intentional wrong such as fraud."].)

Plaintiff's prayer for attorney's fees is groundless because she has not alleged any statutory or contractual basis for recovering attorneys' fees. Assuming Plaintiff's reference to "matters of public concern" is intended to invoke the private attorney general statute, Code of Civil Procedure section 1021.5, that statute authorizes an award of attorney's fees to successful parties only in actions that have "resulted in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest." It is essential to such an award, among other things, that "a significant benefit, whether pecuniary or nonpecuniary, has been conferred on the general public or a large class of persons." (Code Civ. Proc. 1021.5(a).) This is an action for medical malpractice and related claims brought on behalf of an individual plaintiff, which as currently pled cannot possibly meet that and other statutory requirements for an award of attorneys' fees.

Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
